facts_disclosed: Moved to a bigger apartment in Villeray — needed more space and a real kitchen

turns.content: so I'm sitting in my new apartment surrounded by boxes and I just made borscht and I'm crying a little bit. it's fine. everything is fine
Crying over borscht in a new apartment sounds like it's either very fine or very not fine — which one is it? And congratulations on the move! When did this happen?

no no it's the good kind of crying. happy crying. I moved last weekend — finally got out of my old place. I'm in Villeray now and the apartment is... mon Dieu. it's actually big enough to live in. like a real adult person lives here. and the KITCHEN. I have actual counter space. I have a gas stove. I can open a drawer without bumping into the fridge
A gas stove and real counter space — for someone who cooks for a living, that must feel like a revelation. What made you decide to make the move? Was the old place just too cramped?

I loved the Plateau, I really did. the neighbourhood was beautiful, I could walk to work, the energy was great. but my apartment there was a joke. the kitchen was smaller than my station at work — I am not exaggerating. I couldn't prep anything properly at home. if I wanted to make a meal I had to use the cutting board balanced on the stove because there was no counter. a chef who can't cook at home, c'est ridicule

That's an absurd irony — spending your professional life in a real kitchen and then coming home to a space where you can't even properly use a cutting board. So Villeray gave you more space for a reasonable price?

oui, the rent is actually not much more than what I was paying in the Plateau, which is insane because I got twice the space. Villeray is still kind of under the radar — lots of young families, quieter, not as hip as the Plateau but I don't need hip. I need a kitchen where I can actually move. and this place has big windows that face south so the light in the morning is just... parfait

South-facing light and a real kitchen at a reasonable price — that's a serious upgrade. It sounds like you traded neighbourhood energy for quality of life at home, which seems like the right call for someone who spends most of their waking hours at work anyway. So tell me about this borscht that made you cry.

ok so it was the first thing I made. first night, everything still in boxes, I had nothing unpacked except my knives and one pot. I found beets at the depanneur around the corner and I just... started. Babushka's recipe. the one I know by heart. and standing there in this new kitchen with a real stove, stirring borscht, with enough room to actually turn around without hitting a wall — I don't know. something broke open

Making your grandmother's borscht as the first meal in a new kitchen is such a meaningful way to christen the space. It's like you were planting a flag — this kitchen is yours now, and it starts with the recipe that started everything. What broke open — was it relief? Nostalgia? Both?

both, I think. and something else. like... I've been a professional cook for years but I never had a home kitchen that felt like a real kitchen. it was always this split — serious cooking at work, survival cooking at home. and now I can actually develop recipes here, test things, make the food I grew up with properly. the borscht tasted different when I had space to make it right. more care, more attention to each step. I even had room to let the beets roast first instead of just boiling everything like I had to in the old place

Having the space to roast the beets properly instead of cutting corners — that's the difference between cooking out of necessity and cooking with intention. It sounds like this apartment is going to change your whole relationship with food at home. Are you planning to use it for recipe development too?

absolutely. I already have ideas. that duck confit I've been working on, I can properly test variations at home now instead of sneaking time at the restaurant. and I want to do more Babushka recipes — pirozhki, proper blini, maybe even attempt her Napoleon cake which takes about fourteen hours and three hundred layers of puff pastry. I never had the counter space before. now I do

A fourteen-hour Napoleon cake is the kind of project that needs a proper kitchen — both the counter space and the emotional space to commit to something that ambitious. It sounds like this move is unlocking a whole side of your cooking that was dormant. The Villeray apartment isn't just bigger, it's giving you permission to cook the way you've always wanted to at home.

putain, exactly. that's exactly it. ok I'm going to eat my borscht now and unpack approximately one box before I pass out. moving is exhausting. but the good kind of exhausting. like after a really good service — you're dead but you're satisfied. merci for listening to me be emotional about soup

Being emotional about soup in a new kitchen is one of the most valid things I've heard. Enjoy the borscht, unpack at your own pace, and welcome to Villeray. That apartment sounds like it was waiting for you. Bonne nuit — and congratulations on the move.
